name: 温度与温度计
subject: physics
curriculum_points: 了解液体温度计的工作原理。会用常见温度计测量温度。能说出生活环境中常见的温度值。
会正确使用天平、温度计等实验器材，能按实验方案操作，获得实验数据。

excerpts.text: 了解液体温度计的工作原理。会用常见温度计测量温度。能说出生活环境中常见的温度值。
会正确使用天平、温度计等实验器材，能按实验方案操作，获得实验数据。

interactive_resources.description: GeoGebra 物理公式可视化：调节参数观察物理量关系

supplements.curriculum_md_raw:
# 温度与温度计

## 基本信息
- **学科**: 物理
- **学段**: 初中
- **年级**: 八年级
- **章节**: 热学
- **知识点**: 温度与温度计
- **课标版本**: 义务教育课程标准（2022年版）

---

## 一、课标原文

### 1.1 内容要求
>
1.1.2 了解液体温度计的工作原理。会用常见温度计测量温度。能说出生活环境中常见的温度值，尝试对环境温度问题发表自己的见解。

例1 尝试对温室效应、热岛效应等发表自己的见解。
>
---
>
### 1.2 学业要求
>
同上（属于1.1物态变化的学业要求范围）
>
---
>
### 1.3 教学提示
>
同上
---

## 二、教学目标

### 2.1 知识与技能
- 学生能够理解温度与温度计的基本概念和原理
- 学生能够正确进行相关计算和推理
- 学生能够识别和描述相关的概念、现象或材料
- 学生能够理解温度与温度计的基本概念和原理

### 2.2 过程与方法
- 学生经历通过自主探究和合作交流，掌握学习物理的方法
- 学生能够运用物理学科方法解决问题
- 学生能够在交流中表达自己的思路
- 学生经历通过自主探究和合作交流，掌握学习物理的方法

### 2.3 情感态度与价值观
- 学生感受物理与现实生活密切相关
- 学生体验物理探究的乐趣，树立学好数学的信心
- 学生养成认真细致、独立思考的学习习惯
- 学生感受物理与现实生活密切相关

---

## 三、教学重难点

### 3.1 教学重点
- 温度与温度计的基本内容和基本方法
- 形成完整的知识体系结构
- 温度与温度计的基本内容和基本方法
- 形成完整的知识体系结构

### 3.2 教学难点
- 掌握温度与温度计的基本运算和简单应用
- 建立知识之间的联系，实现知识的迁移应用
- 掌握温度与温度计的基本运算和简单应用
- 建立知识之间的联系，实现知识的迁移应用

## 五、教学建议（学科化补灌）

### 5.1 教学方法
- 以可观察实验或生活现象引入「温度与温度计」，先让学生描述现象，再提出可检验的问题。
- 强调控制变量、数据记录、图像分析和单位意识，避免只记公式不理解条件。
- 围绕课标要点“了解液体温度计的工作原理。会用常见温度计测量温度。能说出生活环境中常见的温度值。”，设计预测—实验—解释—迁移的学习链条。

deep_textbook_snippets.text: . Let us use the subscript 1 for the moon and the subscript 2 for the satellite. We are asked to find T2. The given information tells us that the orbital radius of the moon is , and that the period of the moon is . The height of the artificial satellite above Earth’s surface is given, so to get the distance r2 from the center of Earth we must add the height to the radius of Earth (6380 km). This gives . Now all quantities are known, so T2 can be found. Solution To solve for T2, we cross-multiply and take the square root, yielding Discussion This is a reasonable period for a satellite in a fairly low orbit. It is interesting that any satellite at this altitude will complete one orbit in the same amount of time. Practice Problems 1. A planet with no axial tilt is located in another solar system. It circles its sun in a very elliptical orbit so that the temperature varies greatly throughout the year. If the year there has 612 days and the inhabitants celebrate the coldest day on day 1 of their calendar, when is the warmest day? a. Day 1 b. Day 153 c. Day 306 d. Day 459 2. A geosynchronous Earth satellite is one that has an orbital period of precisely 1 day. Such orbits are useful for communication and weather observation because the satellite remains above the same point on Earth (provided it orbits in the equatorial plane in the same direction as Earth’s rotation). The ratio for the moon is . Calculate the radius of the orbit of such a satellite. a. b. c. d. Check Your Understanding 3. Are Kepler’s laws purely descriptive, or do they contain causal information? a. Kepler’s la

The RHIC Collider Figure 10.9 shows the Brookhaven National Laboratory in Upton, NY. The circular structure houses a particle accelerator called the RHIC, which stands for Relativistic Heavy Ion Collider. The heavy ions in the name are gold nuclei that have been stripped of their electrons. Streams of ions are accelerated in several stages before entering the big ring seen in the figure. Here, they are accelerated to their final speed, which is about 99.7 percent the speed of light. Such high speeds are called relativistic. All the relativistic phenomena we have been discussing in this chapter are very pronounced in this case. At this speed = 12.9, so that relativistic time dilates by a factor of about 13, and relativistic length contracts by the same factor. Figure 10.9 Brookhaven National Laboratory. The circular structure houses the RHIC. (energy.gov, Wikimedia Commons) Two ion beams circle the 2.4-mile long track around the big ring in opposite directions. The paths can then be made to cross, thereby causing ions to collide. The collision event is very short-lived but amazingly intense. The temperatures and pressures produced are greater than those in the hottest suns. At 4 trillion degrees Celsius, this is the hottest material ever created in a 10.4 10.2 • Consequences of Special Relativity
> **[Figure 193, Page 331]** A figure or diagram (300×300px) related to: energy, simply, correct, form, Einstein, famous. This image appears on page 331 of the textbook.
![Figure 193 - A figure or diagram (300×300px) related to: energy, simply, ](OpenStax_HighSchool_Physics_images/i

![Figure 196 - A figure or diagram (299×334px) related to: Figure, fireplac](OpenStax_HighSchool_Physics_images/img_0196_c8551e7f.png)
> **[Figure 197, Page 349]** A figure or diagram (300×370px) related to: Figure, fireplace, heat, transfer, occurs, three. This image appears on page 349 of the textbook.
![Figure 197 - A figure or diagram (300×370px) related to: Figure, fireplac](OpenStax_HighSchool_Physics_images/img_0197_c8551e7f.png)
surface. In contrast, a particle in the higher-temperature region (left side) has more kinetic energy before collision, but its energy decreases after colliding with the contact surface. Convection is heat transfer by the movement of a fluid. This type of heat transfer happens, for example, in a pot boiling on the stove, or in thunderstorms, where hot air rises up to the base of the clouds.

In everyday language, the term fluid is usually taken to mean liquid. For example, when you are sick and the doctor tells you to “push fluids,” that only means to drink more beverages—not to breath more air. However, in physics, fluid means a liquid or a gas. Fluids move differently than solid material, and even have their own branch of physics, known as fluid dynamics, that studies how they move. As the temperature of fluids increase, they expand and become less dense. For example, Figure 11.4 could represent the wall of a balloon with different temperature gases inside the balloon than outside in the environment. The hotter and thus faster moving gas particles inside the balloon strike the surface with more force than the cooler air outside, causing the balloon to expand. This decrease in density relative to its environment creates buoyancy (the tendency to rise). Convection is driven by buoyancy—hot air rises because it is less dense than the surrounding air. Sometimes, we control the temperature of our homes or ourselves by controlling air movement. Sealing leaks around doors with weather stripping keeps out the cold wind in winter. The house in Figure 11.5 and the pot of water on the stove in Figure 11.6 are both examples of convection and buoyancy by human design. Ocean currents and large-scale atmospheric circulation transfer energy from one part of the globe to another, and are examples of natural convection. Figure 11.5 Air heated by the so-called gravity furnace expands and rises, forming a convective loop that transfers energy to other parts of the room. As the a

deep_worked_example.stem: 根据教材片段，设计一道关于「温度与温度计」的现象解释或实验探究题。
deep_worked_example.solution_outline: 先描述现象或实验条件，再指出关键概念和证据，最后给出解释或结论。
deep_worked_example.evidence_excerpt: . Let us use the subscript 1 for the moon and the subscript 2 for the satellite. We are asked to find T2. The given information tells us that the orbital radius of the moon is , and that the period of the moon is . The height of the artificial satellite above

errors.description: 只套用「温度与温度计」相关公式，忽略物理情境、单位和适用条件。
实验分析时没有控制变量，导致无法判断因果关系。

exercises.stem: 围绕「温度与温度计」，写出一个可观察现象，并说明其中涉及的物理量或规律。
exercises.answer: 应扣住课标要点：了解液体温度计的工作原理。会用常见温度计测量温度。能说出生活环境中常见的温度值。，说明现象、条件和规律。

exercises.stem: 如果要验证「温度与温度计」相关规律，你会控制哪些变量？记录哪些数据？
exercises.answer: 需说明实验目的、控制变量、测量数据、可能误差和结论依据。

real_world: 观察家用电器、交通工具、运动器材或自然现象中与「温度与温度计」相关的物理规律。
